tweets.content:
Well Well Well!

People have sent me multiple videos since I made that post. What is the solution for this? They seem to have absolutely no fear of anyone, theft of public property in broad daylight.


Video
Elite Predators (@elitepredatorss)

Met someone last evening. The guy is into the waste processing business and was earlier in the scrap collection business. I am generally very curious about how different businesses work and what kind of potential they have in the future. Heard some shocking facts:

- A seasoned mid-level scrap collector can earn up to ₹10K–₹12K per day. The game is to build a network of other waste collectors who can supply good-quality scrap.

- The industry is booming, and businesses in it are extremely cash-rich. You’re looking at millionaire-level annual numbers here.

I have always had a theory in my mind: are there groups across countries motivated to damage infrastructure and steal public assets meant for citizens’ safety and convenience?

Things like construction materials, especially iron, bridge railings, reflectors, metal signboards, benches at bus stops.

He didn’t confirm it outright, but he seemed uncomfortable. After gently pushing for a couple of minutes, he partially acknowledged it and said such activities can be politically motivated, and that if one digs deeper, a religious angle might also emerge.

📷Picture for representation

tweets.title:
UK Sending Warship to West Asia Ahead of Hormuz Mission
The UK will send a destroyer to West Asia ahead of any international mission to help protect shipping in the key Strait of Hormuz, its defense ministry said Saturday.

“The pre-positioning of HMS Dragon is part of prudent planning that will ensure that the UK is ready, as part of a multinational coalition jointly led by the UK and France, to secure the strait, when conditions allow,” a ministry spokesperson told AFP.

Britain and France said last month that military plans to secure the Strait of Hormuz were coming together and would succeed in restoring trade flows through the vital passage.

The MoD said deploying HMS Dragon would strengthen the confidence of commercial shipping and support mine clearance efforts once hostilities end.

Today, a year earlier.

When Pakistanis thought that they would send a few missiles to New Delhi and attack Harmandir Sahab and blame India for the attack on one of the holiest sites of Sikhs.

India unleashed merely a spec of its inventory of hell, less than 50 munitions and made them kneel in hours.

After 1971, that was the fastest surrender of Pakistan. A new record for India. First fastest was 13 days in 1971, this one came in at around 88 hours.

The next surrender will come within the first couple of hours. The most logical thing would be to keep that hotline ringing till we sort every major military establishment inside Pakistan.

We can't kill the snake, but we can defang it to get 10-15 years of uninterrupted focus on national growth.

Defence Research & Development Laboratory DRDL of DRDO on Saturday achieved a path-breaking milestone in the development of hypersonic missiles by successfully conducting an extensive long-duration test of its actively cooled full scale scramjet combustor.

A run-time of over 1,200 seconds was achieved at the state-of-the-art scramjet connect pipe test (SCPT) facility in Hyderabad, building upon the earlier successful test of over 700 seconds conducted in January this year.

This successful test positions India at the forefront of advanced aerospace capabilities and continuously emerging war technologies. The remarkable feat is achieved through cutting-edge supersonic air-breathing engine, which uses indigenously developed liquid hydrocarbon endothermic fuel, high temperature thermal barrier coating and advanced manufacturing processes. 

The ground-tests conducted at the SCPT facility have successfully validated the design of advanced active cooled scramjet combustor as well as the capabilities of  test facility.

tweets.title:
Who is India's next CDS, Lt. Gen. NS Raja Subramani?

Commissioned into the Garhwal Rifles in December 1985, Lt Gen Subramani has a career spanning over 37 years in the Indian Army. 
Over the course of his service, he has served across a wide range of conflict zones and terrains. He has held several Command, Staff and Instructional appointments.

Lt Gen Subramani is a graduate of the National Defence Academy (NDA) and the Indian Military Academy (IMA).
He has also attended the Joint Services Command and Staff College in Bracknell, United Kingdom, and the National Defence College in New Delhi.

He also holds a Master of Arts degree from King's College London and an MPhil in Defence Studies from Madras University. 

He has been awarded some of India’s highest military honours:
Param Vishisht Seva Medal (PVSM)
Ati Vishisht Seva Medal (AVSM)
Sena Medal
Vishisht Seva Medal

Lieutenant General NS Raja Subramani is currently serving as the Military Adviser at the National Security Council Secretariat, a position he has held since September 1, 2025.
Before this, he served as the Vice Chief of the Army Staff from July 1, 2024, to July 31, 2025.

With this successful trial of Agni MIRV, India once again demonstrated the capability to target multiple strategic targets using a single missile system. 

The telemetry and tracking was carried out by multiple ground and ship-based stations. These systems tracked the entire missile trajectory from lift-off till the impact of all payloads. Flight data confirmed that all mission objectives were met during the trial.

Video
Defence Decode® (@DefenceDecode)

India successfully tested an advanced Agni missile with MIRV capability from Dr APJ Abdul Kalam Island, Odisha on May 08, 2026 🇮🇳

The missile reportedly carried multiple independently targetable payloads (#MIRV) against geographically distributed targets across the Indian Ocean Region.

MIRV capability is a major strategic leap. Instead of a single warhead, one missile can deploy multiple independently guided re-entry vehicles toward separate targets, significantly complicating enemy missile defence systems.

The test also validates advances in:
• Post-boost vehicle control
• Re-entry vehicle separation
• Precision guidance
• Long-range mission management
A major milestone in India’s strategic deterrence architecture.

tweets.title:
China Confirms On‑Ground Role In Supporting Pakistan During Operation Sindoor

China has, for the first time, officially admitted that its personnel were present on Pakistani soil during Operation Sindoor, providing direct technical support to the Pakistan Air Force.

This revelation came through China’s state broadcaster CCTV, where engineers from the Aviation Industry Corporation of China (AVIC) described their involvement in supporting Pakistani operations. 

Zhang Heng, an engineer from AVIC’s Chengdu Aircraft Design and Research Institute, recounted his experiences at Pakistani air bases during the conflict. He described the constant roar of fighter jets, the wail of air‑raid sirens, and the extreme heat that reached 50 degrees Celsius by late morning.

The admission marks a significant moment in the India‑Pakistan conflict narrative, as Beijing had previously refrained from acknowledging any on‑ground role in the confrontation.

tweets.title:
For a glide weapon kit like TARA, loft/toss optimisation and high-altitude release envelopes are critical because the weapon has no propulsion of its own. Its range depends on how efficiently it converts the launch aircraft’s speed, altitude, and release geometry into glide energy.

#TARA can reportedly be released across a wide envelope - from low-level tactical ingress (~10,000 ft) to high-alti stand-off attacks (~45,000 ft). A release from 45,000 ft can potentially double the effective range compared to low-alti release, helping push the weapon into the 80 km+ class.

Its flight computer and control laws are designed for toss profiles, allowing aircraft to briefly climb and “throw” the weapon rather than flying directly toward the target. Combined with ±30° off-bore release capability, this enables survivable stand-off attack geometry.

In practical terms, an aircraft could approach through a valley, briefly climb, release TARA in a loft profile, and immediately bank away while the weapon autonomously glides toward the target.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Day 3 Outcome

Day 3 witnessed intense drone duels and the expansion of the conflict into the maritime domain as India enforced multi-domain pressure on Pakistan.
1. Air Defence Mastery: Indian forces achieved near-total interception of over 600 Pakistani drones. The IACCS system proved highly effective, integrating multiple sensors and platforms to maintain a robust air defence shield along the IB and LoC.
2. Naval Compellence: The forward deployment of the INS Vikrant Carrier Battle Group into the northern Arabian Sea created a powerful deterrent. By positioning strike assets within range of Karachi Port, India imposed serious economic and strategic pressure on Pakistan’s primary maritime lifeline.
3. Strategic Signalling: India demonstrated its ability to operate simultaneously across land, air, and sea domains. The naval posturing added a new layer of compellence, pinning Pakistani naval forces and limiting their options for escalation.
4. Diplomatic Resolve: PM Modi’s firm response to the US Vice President sent a clear message — India will not be deterred and remains prepared to escalate on its own terms if Pakistan continues its aggression.
